narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 94.4% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows solid choices like planning to study full-time and coming from a middle-income family, but it also highlights some key academic hurdles: your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 sits below the typical 3.21 average, your LSAT score of 28 is well under the usual 36.7, and your school deciles—2nd for Year 1 grades (versus an average 6th) and 1st for Year 3 (far below the average 6th)—stand out as particularly low compared to most applicants. As a male Asian student, you're in line with common demographics, where males make up about 56% and Asians around 6% of the pool, mostly full-timers like you. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 decile rank, which had the biggest influence in pushing the prediction toward failure. Right behind that was your LSAT score, followed closely by your Year 1 decile, each adding weight to the risk of not passing. Your family income level, racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender rounded out the influences in that order, all nudging the outlook toward struggle rather than success when stacked against typical students who pass. None of these factors pulled in a positive direction for you; instead, they collectively painted a picture riskier than the average applicant who tends to have stronger grades and test scores. To turn this around for future applications or bar prep, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim above 36, build a higher GPA through post-bac courses or a master's, and prioritize consistent academic performance early, as those Year 1 and 3 deciles were pivotal red flags. Many students in similar spots have improved by targeting test prep programs or academic coaching tailored to law school rigor. With targeted effort on these, you could shift your profile closer to successful peers and boost your predicted odds significantly. Hang in there—this is a roadmap, not a dead end.
